note: Practice questions authored by Fly GACA from the GACAR library for study. Not actual GACA exam questions. Every answer cites its source — verify against the official GACAR.

banks.title: Instrument Rating Requirements
banks.desc: Eligibility, experience and currency for the instrument rating under GACAR Part 61.
banks.source: GACAR Part 61 (Instrument Rating) · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 61, to apply for an instrument rating a person must, at minimum —
options: Hold at least a current private pilot certificate (or be concurrently applying for one) with an appropriate aircraft rating
Hold only a student pilot certificate
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(a)(1) requires an instrument-rating applicant to hold at least a current private pilot certificate — or be concurrently applying for one — with an airplane, helicopter, or powered-lift rating appropriate to the instrument rating sought.

questions.q: Under §61.89, an instrument-rating applicant who does not already hold one must pass a knowledge test on the aeronautical knowledge areas of —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(a)(2) requires the applicant (unless already instrument-rated) to pass the knowledge test on the aeronautical knowledge areas of Appendix B to GACAR Part 141.

questions.q: Under §61.89, no person may act as PIC of a civil aircraft under IFR, or in weather less than the minimums for VFR flight, unless that person holds —
options: An appropriate category/class/type rating and an instrument rating (or equivalent ATP privileges)
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(b) bars acting as PIC under IFR or in below-VFR weather unless the pilot holds the appropriate category, class, and type rating plus an instrument rating for the aircraft (or the equivalent ATP privileges).

questions.q: To act as PIC under IFR, §61.17 requires that within the preceding period the pilot have performed and logged, among other tasks, at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3) requires, for instrument recent experience, at least six instrument approaches (plus holding procedures and course tracking) performed solely by reference to instruments.

questions.q: Under §61.17, the instrument approaches, holding, and course-tracking tasks required to act as PIC under IFR (in an aircraft) must have been performed within the preceding —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3)(i) sets the instrument recent-experience window at the 6 months preceding the month of the flight.

questions.q: Which task is part of the instrument recent experience §61.17 requires to act as PIC under IFR?
options: Intercepting and tracking courses using electronic navigation systems
A biennial flight review in a glider
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3) lists intercepting and tracking courses using electronic navigation systems (along with six instrument approaches and holding procedures) as an instrument recent-experience task.

questions.q: Under §61.17, a pilot who has not met the instrument experience requirements for more than six calendar months may regain instrument currency only by —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(4) provides that once the instrument experience lapse exceeds six calendar months, currency can be reestablished only by completing an instrument proficiency check.

questions.q: Under §61.17, an instrument proficiency check must consist of —
options: The areas of operation and instrument tasks required in the instrument rating practical test standards
A short oral quiz on weather theory
The night takeoff and landing recency tasks
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(4) states the instrument proficiency check must consist of the areas of operation and instrument tasks required in the instrument rating practical test standards.

questions.q: Under §61.17, an instrument proficiency check may be given by all of the following EXCEPT —
options: Any private pilot who happens to hold an instrument rating
An authorized company check pilot under GACAR Part 121, 125, or 135
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(4)(ii) lists who may give the check — an examiner, an authorized instructor, an authorized company check pilot, or a person approved by the President — but not simply any instrument-rated private pilot.

questions.q: Under §61.89, if instrument-rating privileges are to be exercised in multi-engine aircraft, the flight training must include operating the aircraft solely by reference to instruments —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(c) requires that multi-engine instrument training include operating the aircraft of the appropriate category solely by reference to instruments with one engine inoperative.

questions.q: Under §61.89, a person holding or applying for an instrument rating on a private pilot certificate must hold a Class 1 medical certificate or —
options: Obtain a waiver from the President
Have established hearing acuity meeting the Class 1 medical hearing requirements
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(d) requires either a Class 1 medical certificate or established hearing acuity based on compliance with the hearing requirements for issue of a Class 1 medical certificate.

questions.q: Under §61.89, to act as PIC of a glider under IFR a person must hold a pilot certificate with a glider category rating and —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(b)(3) allows a glider to be flown under IFR by a person holding a pilot certificate with a glider category rating and an airplane instrument rating.

questions.q: Under §61.19, a person may serve as SIC of an aircraft under IFR only if, among other requirements, that person holds —
options: An instrument rating or privilege applicable to the aircraft being flown
A type rating in every aircraft in the fleet
questions.explain: GACAR §61.19(a)(2) requires an SIC to hold an instrument rating, or privilege applicable to the aircraft being flown, when the flight is conducted under IFR.

questions.q: Under §61.89, an instrument-rating applicant must pass a practical test on the areas of operation prescribed in —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(a)(4) requires the applicant to pass the practical test on the areas of operation set out in Appendix B to GACAR Part 141.

questions.q: Under §61.17, when instrument recent experience is accomplished in an aviation training device (ATD), the required tasks must have been performed within the preceding —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3) provides a shorter window for ATD-based instrument recency: the required tasks (including 3 hours and six approaches) must be within the 2 months preceding the month of the flight.

questions.q: Under §61.89, which is an acceptable way to meet the instrument-rating training requirement?
options: Simply holding a valid medical certificate
Graduating from an approved instrument rating course under Appendix B to GACAR Part 141
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(a)(3) lists acceptable training routes, including a certificate of graduation from an approved instrument rating certification course under Appendix B to GACAR Part 141.

questions.q: The instrument recent-experience tasks in §61.17 must be performed —
options: Solely by reference to instruments, in actual or simulated instrument flight conditions
Only in actual IMC with a safety pilot
Only in a full flight simulator
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3) requires the recent-experience tasks to be performed solely by reference to instruments, in actual or simulated instrument flight conditions.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 61, an instrument rating is required in order to —
options: Carry passengers for hire in day VFR
Act as PIC under IFR or in weather below the minimums for VFR flight
Fly a single-engine airplane in day VFR
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(b) makes an instrument rating a prerequisite to acting as PIC under IFR or in weather conditions less than the minimums prescribed for VFR flight.

questions.q: Which of the following is one of the recent-experience tasks §61.17 requires to act as PIC under IFR?
options: A power-off stall series in VMC
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3) lists holding procedures and tasks, along with six instrument approaches and course tracking, as instrument recent-experience requirements.

questions.q: Under §61.17, an instrument proficiency check (for other than a glider) may be conducted in an aircraft appropriate to the category or in —
options: An FSTD representative of the aircraft category
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(4)(i) allows the instrument proficiency check to be conducted in an aircraft appropriate to the category or, for other than a glider, in an FSTD that is representative of the aircraft category.

questions.q: Under §61.89, an applicant for an instrument rating who does not yet hold a private pilot certificate may —
options: Apply for the instrument rating concurrently with the private pilot certificate
Never be issued an instrument rating
Skip the private pilot requirement entirely
Obtain it only after logging 1,500 hours
questions.explain: GACAR §61.89(a)(1) permits an applicant to be concurrently applying for the private pilot certificate (with the appropriate aircraft rating) while seeking the instrument rating.

banks.title: IFR Flight Rules
banks.desc: Instrument flight rules: clearances, minima, fuel and alternate requirements under GACAR Part 91.
banks.source: GACAR Part 91, Subpart B/E · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.181, after flying to the first aerodrome of intended landing and then to the alternate, an aeroplane operating under IFR must carry enough fuel to fly for a further —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.181(a)(3) requires an aeroplane under IFR to carry fuel to reach the destination and alternate and then fly a further 45 minutes at normal cruising speed.

questions.q: For a rotorcraft operating under IFR, the final reserve fuel required by GACAR §91.181 (after destination and alternate) is enough to fly for —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.181(a)(3) sets the rotorcraft final reserve at 30 minutes at normal cruising speed, versus 45 minutes for other aircraft.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.183, no person may operate an aircraft in controlled airspace under IFR unless the PIC has —
options: Filed a VFR flight plan and obtained a weather briefing
An instrument rating and a working transponder only
Filed an IFR flight plan and received an appropriate ATC clearance
questions.explain: GACAR §91.183 requires both a filed IFR flight plan and receipt of an appropriate ATC clearance before operating IFR in controlled airspace.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.135, each person operating an aircraft in Class A airspace must —
options: Operate under either VFR or IFR
Operate under VFR with an ATC clearance
questions.explain: GACAR §91.135 requires all operations in Class A airspace to be conducted under IFR, under an ATC clearance received before entering the airspace.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.135, operations in Class A airspace may be conducted only under an ATC clearance received —
options: At any time during the flight
questions.explain: GACAR §91.135(a) states that Class A operations may be conducted only under an ATC clearance received prior to entering the airspace.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.77, an aircraft must be operated under IFR whenever it is at or above —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.77 makes IFR mandatory for any aircraft operating at flight level 150 or above.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.181, an alternate aerodrome need not be included in the IFR fuel calculation for an aeroplane if a Part 97 approach is published at the destination and, for at least 1 hour before and after ETA, the ceiling and visibility will be at least —
options: 2,000 ft above the aerodrome elevation and 5 km
questions.explain: GACAR §91.181(b) waives the alternate-fuel requirement for aeroplanes when a Part 97 approach exists and forecasts show a ceiling at least 2,000 ft above aerodrome elevation and visibility at least 5 km for the hour before and after ETA.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.185, an alternate aerodrome must be listed in an IFR flight plan unless —
options: The flight is shorter than 100 NM
A Part 97 instrument approach is available at the destination and forecast weather meets the specified ceiling/visibility minima
The pilot holds an airline transport pilot licence
questions.explain: GACAR §91.185(b) allows omission of an alternate only when an approved Part 97 approach is published for the destination and the forecast ceiling and visibility meet the stated minimums around ETA.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.185(c), the standard alternate-aerodrome minimums for a precision approach are —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.185(c) sets the standard precision-approach alternate minimums (for aircraft other than rotorcraft) at a 600 ft ceiling and 3,200 m visibility.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.185(c), the standard alternate-aerodrome minimums for a nonprecision approach or APV are —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.185(c) sets the standard nonprecision/APV alternate minimums (for aircraft other than rotorcraft) at an 800 ft ceiling and 3,200 m visibility.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.187, a VOR used for IFR navigation must have been operationally checked within the preceding —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.187(a)(2) permits IFR use of a VOR only if it was operationally checked within the preceding 30 days and found within tolerance.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.187, the maximum permissible indicated bearing error when checking VOR equipment against a ground test signal or a designated surface checkpoint is —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.187(b) sets the maximum permissible bearing error at ±4° when using a ground test signal or a designated VOR surface checkpoint.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.187, when a VOR is checked against a designated airborne checkpoint, the maximum permissible bearing error is —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.187(b)(3) allows a wider ±6° tolerance when using an airborne VOR checkpoint, compared with ±4° for ground checks.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.187, when checking one VOR system against another (dual VOR), the maximum permissible variation between the two indicated bearings is —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.187(c) permits checking one VOR against the other tuned to the same station, with a maximum permissible variation of 4° between the two indicated bearings.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.199, over a designated mountainous zone where no minimum altitude is prescribed, the minimum IFR altitude is —
options: 1,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of course
2,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of course
1,500 ft above the highest obstacle within 5 NM of course
2,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 5 NM of course
questions.explain: GACAR §91.199(a)(2)(i) requires, over mountainous terrain with no prescribed minimum, an altitude of 2,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of the course flown.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.199, over non-mountainous terrain where no minimum altitude is prescribed, the minimum IFR altitude is —
options: 500 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of course
1,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of course
1,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 5 NM of course
2,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of course
questions.explain: GACAR §91.199(a)(2)(ii) requires, in non-mountainous areas with no prescribed minimum, an altitude of 1,000 ft above the highest obstacle within 4 NM of the course flown.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.201, a PIC in level cruising flight under IFR in controlled airspace must maintain —
options: The semicircular cruising altitude for the magnetic course
The altitude or flight level assigned by ATC
questions.explain: GACAR §91.201(a) requires a PIC operating IFR in controlled airspace to maintain the altitude or flight level assigned by ATC.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.201, under IFR in uncontrolled airspace below 13,000 ft MSL on a magnetic course of 0° through 179°, the PIC must fly —
options: The altitude last assigned by ATC
questions.explain: GACAR §91.201(b) requires, in uncontrolled airspace below 13,000 ft MSL, any odd thousand-foot MSL altitude on magnetic courses of 0° through 179°.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.205, when making an initial approach to a radio navigation facility under IFR, the PIC may not descend below the pertinent minimum altitude for initial approach until —
options: Arrival over that facility has been definitely established
questions.explain: GACAR §91.205 prohibits descent below the minimum initial-approach altitude until arrival over the facility has been definitely established.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.209, if two-way radio communications fail under IFR and VMC are encountered, the PIC should —
options: Continue to the destination on the flight plan regardless of conditions
Continue the flight under VFR and land at the nearest suitable aerodrome
Climb to FL 150 and enter a holding pattern
questions.explain: GACAR §91.209(b) directs a PIC who loses radio in, or encounters, VMC to continue under VFR and land at the nearest suitable aerodrome, reporting arrival to ATS.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.211, a change from IFR to VFR flight —
options: May be initiated by ATC on the pilot's behalf
May be initiated only by the PIC, by stating 'CANCELING MY IFR FLIGHT'
Is prohibited once the aircraft is airborne
Requires filing a new flight plan on the ground first
questions.explain: GACAR §91.211(b) provides that only the PIC may initiate a change from IFR to VFR, by stating 'CANCELING MY IFR FLIGHT' along with any flight-plan changes.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.193, if the PIC has not served 100 hours as PIC in the type being flown, the landing minimums (MDA/DA and visibility) are increased by —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.193(a) increases the MDA or DA/DH by 100 ft and the visibility minimum by 800 m for a PIC with fewer than 100 hours as PIC in that aircraft type.

banks.title: Instrument Procedures
banks.desc: Approaches, holding and instrument procedures (GACAR Part 97 and the Saudi AIP ENR).
banks.source: GACAR Part 97 · Saudi AIP (ENR) · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: GACAR Part 97 exists primarily to —
options: Identify the approved instrument flight procedures (IFPs) authorized for use in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
questions.explain: §97.1 states that Part 97 identifies the approved instrument flight procedures (including instrument approach procedures) authorized for use within the territorial limits of the KSA.

questions.q: Under GACAR §97.1, the rules governing the development, validation and approval of instrument flight procedures are addressed under —
questions.explain: §97.1(d) provides that development, validation, approval, promulgation and maintenance of IFPs are addressed under GACAR Part 172, not within Part 97.

questions.q: Per GACAR §97.1, the publication of instrument flight procedures in the KSA AIP and their depiction on aeronautical charts are governed by —
questions.explain: §97.1(e) states the rules for publishing IFPs in the KSA AIP and depicting them on charts are addressed under GACAR Part 175.

questions.q: Under GACAR §97.3, an aircraft approach category is a grouping based on a speed of —
options: Vref (or 1.3 Vso if Vref is not specified) at the maximum certificated landing mass
Vso at the maximum takeoff mass
V2 at the maximum landing mass
questions.explain: §97.3(a) defines the aircraft approach category by a speed of Vref, or, if Vref is not specified, 1.3 Vso at the maximum certificated landing mass.

questions.q: Under the aircraft approach categories in GACAR §97.3, Category A applies to a speed of —
questions.explain: §97.3(a)(1) assigns Category A to aircraft with an approach speed of less than 91 knots indicated airspeed.

questions.q: Under GACAR §97.3, aircraft approach Category C covers a speed of —
options: 166 knots IAS or more but less than 211 knots IAS
questions.explain: §97.3(a)(3) defines Category C as 121 knots or more but less than 141 knots IAS.

questions.q: Per the approach segment definitions in GACAR §97.3, the initial approach segment lies between the initial approach fix and —
options: The intermediate fix (or the point where the aircraft is established on the intermediate course/final approach course)
questions.explain: §97.3(c)(1) defines the initial approach segment as running from the initial approach fix to the intermediate fix, or to where the aircraft is established on the intermediate or final approach course.

questions.q: In GACAR §97.3, the final approach segment of an instrument approach procedure is the segment in which —
options: The aircraft climbs after a missed approach
Alignment and descent for landing are accomplished
The aircraft is vectored to the initial approach fix
questions.explain: §97.3(c)(4) describes the final approach segment as the segment in which alignment and descent for landing are accomplished.

questions.q: According to GACAR §97.3, the missed approach segment of an instrument approach procedure starts at the —
questions.explain: §97.3(c)(5) states the missed approach segment starts at the missed approach point and comprises an initial, intermediate and final phase.

questions.q: Under GACAR §97.3, the intermediate phase of the missed approach extends from the start of climb to the point where obstacle clearance of at least — is first obtained and can be maintained.
questions.explain: §97.3(c)(5)(ii) defines the intermediate phase as extending from the start of climb to where 50 m (164 ft) of obstacle clearance is first obtained and can be maintained.

questions.q: Per GACAR §97.3, a procedure turn is a maneuver in which a turn is made away from a designated track followed by a turn in the opposite direction to —
options: Enter a holding pattern at the fix
Permit the aircraft to intercept and proceed along the reciprocal of the designated track
Establish the aircraft on the missed approach
questions.explain: §97.3(d) defines a procedure turn as a turn away from a designated track and then a turn in the opposite direction so the aircraft can intercept and proceed along the reciprocal of that track.

questions.q: Under GACAR §97.5, all bearings, courses, tracks, headings and radials in the instrument procedures of Part 97 are expressed in —
questions.explain: §97.5(a) provides that all bearings, courses, tracks, headings and radials in Part 97 are in degrees magnetic unless otherwise designated.

questions.q: In the instrument procedures of GACAR Part 97, runway visual range (RVR) values are stated in —
questions.explain: §97.5(b) states RVR values are stated in meters, while other mileages are stated in nautical miles or applicable SI units.

questions.q: Under GACAR §97.11, approved IFPs (including instrument approach and departure procedures) in the KSA are developed and maintained by —
options: Air navigation service providers certificated under GACAR Part 170 providing IFP services under Part 172
questions.explain: §97.11(a) provides that approved IFPs are developed and maintained by air navigation service providers certificated under GACAR Part 170 and providing instrument flight procedure services under GACAR Part 172.

questions.q: According to the Saudi AIP, the transition level (TL) for the whole JEDDAH FIR has been fixed by GACA at —
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 (Transition level) states the TL has been determined by GACA as fixed at FL 150 for the whole of JEDDAH FIR.

questions.q: Per the Saudi AIP, the fixed transition level of FL 150 in JEDDAH FIR is also described as the —
options: Standard cruising level for all IFR flights
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 notes that the transition level, fixed at FL 150, is therefore also the lowest usable flight level for the JEDDAH FIR.

questions.q: Under the Saudi AIP cruising-level rules, en-route cruising levels are expressed in flight levels at and above —
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 (Cruising levels) states levels are expressed as altitude at and below 13,000 FT AMSL, or as flight level at and above FL 150.

questions.q: Per the Saudi AIP, the appropriate flight information service (FIS) frequency for an aerodrome is published —
options: Only in the GEN section of the AIP
At the top of all instrument approach charts
On the en-route chart margins only
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 notes that the appropriate FIS frequency is published at the top of all instrument approach charts.

questions.q: Per the Saudi AIP, a pilot who has not received a landing clearance approaching short final shall —
options: Continue and land, then advise the tower
Request a landing clearance, and if not in receipt of one commence a missed approach not later than the runway landing threshold
Enter a holding pattern over the threshold
Reduce to minimum speed and orbit on final
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 states a landing clearance shall be requested approaching short final, and pilots not in receipt of one shall commence a missed approach not later than the runway landing threshold.

questions.q: Under the Saudi AIP general speed limits, aircraft operated below ALT 10,000 FT anywhere in JEDDAH FIR must not exceed an indicated airspeed of —
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 (Speed limits) sets a maximum of 250 KT IAS below 10,000 FT elsewhere in the FIR (with a lower limit inside the aerodrome traffic circuit).

questions.q: Per the Saudi AIP, the highest altitude at which a VFR flight may operate within JEDDAH FIR is —
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.2 (Maximum level for VFR) states the highest altitude at which a VFR flight may operate within JEDDAH FIR is 12,500 FT.

questions.q: Per the Saudi AIP, an aircraft joining the aerodrome traffic circuit from the same side as the traffic pattern shall normally —
options: Join straight in on the base leg
Arrange flight to join straight in on the downwind leg
Overfly the aerodrome at circuit height then join crosswind
questions.explain: Saudi AIP ENR 1.1 states aircraft approaching from the same side as the traffic pattern shall arrange flight to join straight in on the downwind leg (those from the opposite side cross over the aerodrome at midfield first).

banks.title: Airspace & Air Traffic
banks.desc: ICAO classes A–G, the Jeddah FIR, control areas, speed limits and cruising levels.

questions.q: Which airspace class is the most controlled?
questions.explain: Class A is the most controlled — IFR only, all aircraft separated, continuous ATC service. Class G is the least controlled.

questions.q: A 250 kt IAS speed limit applies below 10,000 ft AMSL in which airspace?
options: Classes C, D, E, F and G
questions.explain: The 250 kt IAS limit below 10,000 ft AMSL applies in Classes C–G.

questions.q: A Control Zone (CTR) is controlled airspace that —
options: Extends upward from a base above the surface
Extends upward from the surface around an aerodrome
questions.explain: A CTR extends from the surface upward to a specified upper limit, protecting traffic arriving at and departing an aerodrome.

questions.q: The airspace of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia is contained within which Flight Information Region?
questions.explain: Saudi airspace is the Jeddah FIR, ICAO indicator OEJD.

questions.q: In Class G airspace, ATC provides —
questions.explain: Class G is uncontrolled — a flight information service (and alerting service) is provided; no separation is given.

questions.q: A Terminal Control Area (TMA) is normally established —
options: At the confluence of ATS routes near one or more major aerodromes
questions.explain: A TMA is a control area at the confluence of ATS routes around one or more major aerodromes, usually based above the surface.

questions.q: Above the transition level, an aircraft's vertical position is expressed as —
options: An altitude on the local QNH
A flight level on the standard pressure setting (1013.2 hPa)
A height above the transition layer
questions.explain: Above the transition level, levels are flown as flight levels referenced to the standard setting of 1013.2 hPa.

questions.q: In Class C airspace, ATC separates —
options: IFR from IFR and from VFR; VFR receives traffic information on other VFR
questions.explain: Class C: IFR is separated from IFR and from VFR; VFR is separated from IFR and given traffic information on other VFR traffic.

questions.q: Designation and classification of airspace in KSA is dealt with in which GACAR Part?
questions.explain: GACAR Part 71 — Designated Airspace, Routes, Points and Minimum Altitudes — covers the designation and classification of airspace.

questions.q: Does a VFR flight need an ATC clearance to operate in Class E airspace?
options: No — clearance is required only for IFR in Class E
questions.explain: In Class E, an ATC clearance and continuous two-way radio are required for IFR; VFR may operate without a clearance.

questions.q: Prohibited, restricted and danger areas are collectively known as —
questions.explain: Prohibited, restricted and danger areas are special-use airspace, depicted on charts and designated under the regulations.

questions.q: The semicircular rule for IFR cruising levels assigns, on a magnetic track of 180°–359° —
options: Any level cleared by ATC only
questions.explain: Under the semicircular rule, magnetic tracks 180°–359° take even flight levels; 0°–179° take odd flight levels. All cruising levels remain subject to ATC clearance.

questions.q: When overlapping airspace designations apply to the same airspace, which operating rules apply?
options: The designation chosen by the pilot
questions.explain: GACAR §71.13 provides that where airspace designations overlap, the operating rules of the more restrictive designation apply.

questions.q: Before operating an aircraft in Class B airspace, the PIC must —
options: Receive an ATC clearance from the facility having jurisdiction
File an IFR flight plan in all cases
questions.explain: GACAR §91.131 requires the PIC to receive an ATC clearance from the ATC facility having jurisdiction before operating in Class B airspace.

questions.q: To take off or land at an aerodrome within Class B airspace, the PIC must hold at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.131 requires the PIC to hold at least a private pilot certificate to take off or land at an aerodrome within a Class B airspace area.

questions.q: Each PIC operating in Class D airspace on an arrival or through flight must establish two-way radio communications with the ATC facility —
options: Prior to entering that airspace, and maintain it while within
questions.explain: GACAR §91.127 requires the PIC to establish two-way radio communications with ATC prior to entering Class D airspace and maintain them while within it.

questions.q: No person may operate an aircraft within a restricted area —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.133 prohibits operating an aircraft within a restricted area contrary to the restrictions imposed; entry to a prohibited area requires the administering authority's permission.

questions.q: Each person operating an aircraft in Class A airspace must operate —
options: Under IFR, with an ATC clearance received prior to entering
questions.explain: GACAR §91.135 requires operations in Class A airspace to be conducted under IFR with an ATC clearance received prior to entering the airspace.

questions.q: A Special VFR (SVFR) clearance permits —
options: VFR flight within a control zone when weather is below normal VMC minima, subject to an ATC clearance
questions.explain: Special VFR lets a flight operate within a control zone in weather below the normal VMC minima, only with an ATC clearance.

questions.q: A Flight Information Region (FIR) is airspace within which —
options: Flight information service and alerting service are provided
questions.explain: An FIR is airspace of defined dimensions within which flight information service and alerting service are provided.

questions.q: ICAO airspace classes are lettered from Class A through —
questions.explain: ICAO defines seven airspace classes, A through G, each with its own rules and services.

questions.q: In Class E airspace, a VFR flight receives —
options: Traffic information as far as practicable, but no separation service
Full separation from all other traffic
No ATC service of any kind
questions.explain: In Class E airspace, VFR flights are given traffic information when practicable, but ATC does not separate them from other traffic.

questions.q: How does a Danger Area differ from a Restricted Area?
options: A Danger Area warns of activities hazardous to flight without legally restricting entry, while a Restricted Area imposes flight restrictions
A Danger Area always prohibits all flight
A Danger Area only exists over water
questions.explain: A Danger Area indicates airspace where activities dangerous to flight may exist, whereas a Restricted Area actually restricts flight under specified conditions.

questions.q: Uncontrolled airspace, where ATC provides no separation service, is designated —
questions.explain: Class G is uncontrolled airspace; ATC provides no separation service, though flight information may be available on request.

banks.title: AIP & Aeronautical Information
banks.desc: The AIP structure, the AIRAC cycle, NOTAMs and how aeronautical information is managed.

questions.q: How long is one AIRAC cycle?
questions.explain: An AIRAC cycle is 28 days. Significant aeronautical changes are grouped onto common, pre-notified AIRAC effective dates.

questions.q: The AIP is divided into which three parts?
questions.explain: An AIP has three parts: GEN (General), ENR (En-route) and AD (Aerodromes).

questions.q: AIRAC effective dates always fall on a —
questions.explain: AIRAC effective dates are 28 days apart and always fall on a Thursday.

questions.q: Where in the AIP would you find the entry for a specific aerodrome?
questions.explain: Aerodrome data is published in the AD (Aerodromes) part of the AIP — e.g. AD 2 for individual aerodromes.

questions.q: A NOTAM is —
options: A permanent change to the AIP
A notice of the establishment, condition or change of any aeronautical facility, service or hazard
questions.explain: A NOTAM gives time-critical notice of the establishment, condition or change of any aeronautical facility, service, procedure or hazard.

questions.q: "AIRAC" stands for —
questions.explain: AIRAC — Aeronautical Information Regulation And Control — is the system of common effective dates for aeronautical information change.

questions.q: The AIP-KSA is published by —
options: The General Authority of Civil Aviation (GACA)
questions.explain: The Aeronautical Information Publication for the Kingdom (AIP-KSA) is published by GACA.

questions.q: En-route information — airways, significant points and en-route airspace — is found in which AIP part?
questions.explain: The ENR (En-route) part of the AIP carries airways, significant points, ATS airspace and en-route procedures.

questions.q: An AIC is an —
questions.explain: An Aeronautical Information Circular (AIC) carries information that does not qualify for the AIP or a NOTAM but is important for safety, navigation or administration.

questions.q: Aeronautical charts and navigation databases are updated on —
questions.explain: Charts and navigation databases update on the AIRAC cycle so that operators and data providers change in step worldwide.

questions.q: Before a flight, perishable safety information such as runway closures is found in —
questions.explain: Time-critical, perishable information — closures, unserviceabilities, hazards — is issued by NOTAM and must be checked before flight.

questions.q: An AIRAC cycle identified as 2605 means —
options: The 5th cycle whose effective date falls in 2026
questions.explain: AIRAC cycles are labelled YYNN — the two-digit year and the ordinal cycle within it. 2605 is the fifth cycle of 2026.

questions.q: The Aeronautical Information Publication (AIP) is organised into which three main parts?
questions.explain: An AIP is divided into GEN (general), ENR (en-route) and AD (aerodromes), per ICAO Annex 15.

questions.q: A NOTAM is:
options: A notice of information essential to operations, distributed by telecommunication
questions.explain: A NOTAM is a notice distributed by telecommunication containing time-critical aeronautical information.

questions.q: Where would you find runway and aerodrome frequency data in the AIP?
questions.explain: Aerodrome data, including runways and frequencies, is in the AD (Aerodromes) section.

questions.q: The ENR section of the AIP contains:
options: En-route information such as airways and airspace
questions.explain: ENR (en-route) holds airway, airspace and en-route navigation information.

questions.q: In the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, the AIP is published by:
options: The State Aeronautical Information Service (GACA AIS)
questions.explain: Each State publishes its AIP through its Aeronautical Information Service; in KSA this is GACA AIS, per ICAO Annex 15.

questions.q: In the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, the transition level is —
options: Set by each pilot before departure
questions.explain: The Saudi AIP states the transition level (TL) has been determined by GACA as fixed at FL 150, with a minimum of 1,000 FT vertical separation above the transition altitude.

questions.q: Unless authorised otherwise, an aircraft operated below 10,000 FT within the Jeddah FIR must not exceed —
questions.explain: The Saudi AIP sets a speed limit of 250 KT (about 288 MPH / 460 KPH) for aircraft operated below ALT 10,000 FT anywhere in the Jeddah FIR, unless authorised to the contrary by the President or ATC.

questions.q: In the Saudi AIP, horizontal distances used for navigation are expressed in —
questions.explain: AIP GEN 2.1 lists the units of measurement: navigation distances are given in nautical miles and tenths, altitudes and elevations in feet, and speeds in knots.

questions.q: According to AIP GEN 2.1, vertical speed (rate of climb or descent) is expressed in —
questions.explain: The AIP units-of-measurement table gives vertical speed in feet per minute, altitudes in feet, and horizontal speed in knots.

questions.q: Within Saudi controlled airspace, a serviceable SSR transponder should have —
options: Mode C selected at all times unless otherwise instructed
The transponder off until requested by ATC
Only Mode A selected below FL 100
questions.explain: AIP ENR 1.6 requires Mode C to be selected at all times unless ATC instructs otherwise, so controllers receive pressure-altitude data.

questions.q: The abbreviation "AFIS" refers to —
questions.explain: AIP GEN 3.3 lists Aerodrome Flight Information Service (AFIS) among the ATS units, providing information and alerting service to aerodrome traffic where no control service is given.

questions.q: Which ICAO document sets the standards for the aeronautical information services that the AIP implements?
questions.explain: ICAO Annex 15 — Aeronautical Information Services — defines how States collect, publish and update aeronautical information, including the AIP, AIRAC and NOTAM systems.

questions.q: An AIP Supplement is used to publish —
options: Permanent changes incorporated into the AIP pages
Temporary changes of long duration (usually three months or more) or extensive text
Short-notice perishable information for immediate action
questions.explain: An AIP Supplement carries temporary changes of long duration (typically three months or more) or information with extensive text/graphics; short-notice perishable items go out by NOTAM, and permanent changes go in an AIP Amendment.

questions.q: The GEN part of the AIP primarily contains —
options: General information: authorities, units of measurement, abbreviations and regulations
questions.explain: GEN (General) holds administrative and general information — responsible authorities, units of measurement, abbreviations, and applicable regulations — while ENR covers en-route and AD covers aerodromes.

questions.q: A "Trigger NOTAM" is issued to —
options: Announce and give brief details of a forthcoming AIP Amendment or Supplement
Close a runway at short notice
Cancel all outstanding NOTAMs at a location
questions.explain: A Trigger NOTAM draws attention to, and gives a short reference for, an AIRAC AIP Amendment or Supplement, so operators know a permanent change is about to take effect.

banks.title: Aircraft, Equipment & Airworthiness
banks.desc: Oxygen, transponders, instrument tests, airworthiness directives and maintenance under GACAR Parts 91, 39 and 43.

questions.q: On a non-pressurized aircraft, above which cabin pressure altitude must the required minimum flight crew use supplemental oxygen for the entire flight time at those altitudes?
questions.explain: GACAR §91.223 requires the minimum flight crew to use supplemental oxygen during the entire flight time at cabin pressure altitudes above 13,000 ft MSL.

questions.q: Between cabin pressure altitudes above 10,000 ft and up to and including 13,000 ft MSL, the required minimum flight crew must use supplemental oxygen for that part of the flight at those altitudes that lasts more than —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.223 requires supplemental oxygen for the minimum flight crew when the flight above 10,000 ft up to 13,000 ft MSL exceeds 30 minutes duration.

questions.q: Within what period must an ATC transponder have been tested and inspected for it to be used as required by GACAR Part 91?
questions.explain: GACAR §91.453 prohibits use of an ATC transponder unless it has been tested and inspected within the preceding 24 months and found to comply with Appendix E to GACAR Part 43.

questions.q: To operate an aircraft in controlled airspace under IFR, the altimeter and static pressure system must have been tested and inspected within —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.451 requires each static pressure system, altimeter and automatic pressure altitude reporting system to have been tested within the preceding 24 months to operate in controlled airspace under IFR.

questions.q: Generally, no person may operate an aircraft unless within the preceding 12 months it has had —
options: An annual inspection in accordance with GACAR Part 43
questions.explain: GACAR §91.449 prohibits operating an aircraft unless within the preceding 12 months it has had an annual inspection in accordance with GACAR Part 43 and been approved for return to service.

questions.q: Who is primarily responsible for maintaining an aircraft in an airworthy condition, including compliance with GACAR Part 39?
options: The owner or operator of the aircraft
questions.explain: GACAR §91.443 states the owner or operator of an aircraft is primarily responsible for maintaining it in an airworthy condition, including compliance with GACAR Part 39.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 39, a person may operate an aircraft to which an airworthiness directive applies —
options: Only in accordance with the requirements of that airworthiness directive
questions.explain: GACAR §39.3 prohibits operating an aircraft to which an airworthiness directive applies except in accordance with the requirements of that directive.

questions.q: If there is a conflict between an airworthiness directive and a related service document, the affected person must —
questions.explain: GACAR §39.7 requires that, in the event of conflict between a service document and an airworthiness directive, the affected person must comply with the airworthiness directive.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 39, a special flight permit may be issued for an aircraft to —
options: Carry passengers for hire despite an open AD
Fly to a repair facility to complete work required by an airworthiness directive, provided it is capable of safe flight
Operate indefinitely with an unsafe condition
questions.explain: GACAR §39.15 allows a special flight permit for an aircraft to fly to a repair facility to complete work required by an AD, provided the aircraft is capable of safe flight.

questions.q: Under GACAR §43.5, the holder of a pilot certificate (other than a sport pilot) may perform which work on an aircraft they own or operate and not used under Part 121 or 135?
questions.explain: GACAR §43.5(g) allows a pilot certificate holder (except a sport pilot) to perform preventive maintenance on an aircraft owned or operated by that pilot and not used under Part 121 or 135.

questions.q: Under GACAR §43.3, an aircraft component may be described as 'rebuilt' only if it has been disassembled, cleaned, inspected, repaired, reassembled and tested —
options: To any standard acceptable to the owner
To the same tolerances and limits as a new item
questions.explain: GACAR §43.3 permits describing an item as rebuilt only if it has been disassembled, cleaned, inspected, repaired, reassembled and tested to the same tolerances and limits as a new item.

questions.q: GACAR §43.19 requires that work done in maintenance, alteration or preventive maintenance leaves the item in a condition —
options: At least equal to its original or properly altered condition
Better than new in all respects
Acceptable to the aircraft owner only
Equal to the minimum equipment list standard
questions.explain: GACAR §43.19 requires the work to be done so the condition of the item is at least equal to its original or properly altered condition with regard to airworthiness.

questions.q: Which instruments are required for VFR day flight?
options: Airspeed indicator, altimeter and magnetic compass
questions.explain: Basic VFR-day equipment under GACAR Part 91 includes an airspeed indicator, altimeter and magnetic compass.

questions.q: An ELT is:
questions.explain: An ELT (Emergency Locator Transmitter) transmits a distress signal to aid search and rescue.

questions.q: Rules for maintenance and return to service are found in GACAR:
questions.explain: Maintenance, preventive maintenance and return to service are governed by GACAR Part 43.

questions.q: The pitot-static system supplies:
options: The airspeed indicator, altimeter and vertical speed indicator
questions.explain: The pitot-static system drives the airspeed indicator, altimeter and VSI.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A blocked pitot tube (with the static port clear) primarily affects the:
questions.explain: A blocked pitot tube directly affects the airspeed indicator.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Continued airworthiness of an aircraft is maintained by:
options: Completing the required inspections and maintenance
questions.explain: Airworthiness is maintained by the inspections and maintenance required under GACAR Part 43.

questions.q: Which of the following is generally required for IFR flight but NOT required for day VFR flight?
options: A gyroscopic attitude/heading system and navigation/radio equipment appropriate to the route
questions.explain: IFR flight requires additional gyroscopic and radio/navigation equipment beyond the basic VFR instrument set.

questions.q: An Airworthiness Directive (AD) is issued when —
options: An unsafe condition exists in a product and is likely to exist in other products of the same design
A new paint scheme is approved
questions.explain: ADs are mandatory corrective actions issued when an unsafe condition is found that likely affects other aircraft/products of the same type design.

questions.q: Preventive maintenance may generally be performed by —
options: The aircraft owner/operator (for permitted items) or an appropriately certificated person
Nobody without a repair station certificate
questions.explain: GACAR Part 43 allows certain simple preventive maintenance tasks to be performed by the owner/operator (if a pilot certificate holder) or other authorized persons, within limits.

questions.q: Maintenance records must be retained to show —
options: The total time in service and the current status of applicable airworthiness directives
questions.explain: Maintenance records must document total time in service and AD compliance status, among other things, so continued airworthiness can be verified.

banks.title: Navigation & Flight Planning
banks.desc: Charts, the coordinate system, magnetic variation and deviation, VOR navigation and wind correction from the FAA Pilot's Handbook.

questions.q: Lines running east and west, parallel to the equator and used to measure position north or south, are called what?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states circles parallel to the equator are parallels of latitude, used to measure degrees of latitude north or south of the equator.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: From which location is longitude measured as the zero (Prime) meridian?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states the Prime Meridian, which passes through Greenwich, England, is the zero line from which longitude is measured east and west to 180 degrees.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: In aerial navigation, what is the angular difference between true direction and magnetic direction called?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states the difference between true and magnetic directions is called variation; isogonic lines join points of equal variation.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A line along which there is zero magnetic variation is known as what?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge explains that along the agonic line the two poles are aligned and there is no variation.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Compass deviation is an error caused by what?
options: The difference between true and magnetic north
The aircraft's own localised magnetic influences acting on the compass
Wind drifting the aircraft off course
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states each aircraft has internal magnetic influences affecting its compass; deviation is corrected using a compass deviation card to obtain the compass course.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Navigating by reference to visible landmarks on the ground is known as what?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge defines pilotage as navigating by reference to visible landmarks, distinct from dead reckoning and radio navigation.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Dead reckoning is best described as which method of navigation?
options: Computing direction and distance from a known position
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge defines dead reckoning as navigation by computations of direction and distance from a known position.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A VOR ground station projects courses outward in all directions; these courses are referred to as what?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge explains a VOR projects straight-line courses called radials in all directions, identified by numbers from 001 to 360 and referenced to magnetic north.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Because VOR transmits on VHF, its signal reception is subject to what limitation?
options: It is restricted to line-of-sight, so range varies with altitude
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge notes VOR signals are VHF and therefore subject to line-of-sight restrictions, so reception range varies in direct proportion to the aircraft's altitude.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: The wind correction angle is applied to do what?
options: Counteract wind drift so the aircraft's track matches the desired course
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge explains the wind correction angle is the number of degrees the heading is offset from the true course to counteract wind drift and keep the track on the desired course.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: What is the scale of a standard sectional aeronautical chart?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states sectional charts have a scale of 1:500,000 (1 inch = about 6.86 NM), allowing detailed information to be shown.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Groundspeed differs from true airspeed in what way?
options: Groundspeed is true airspeed adjusted for wind
Groundspeed is always higher than true airspeed
Groundspeed is true airspeed corrected for temperature
Groundspeed and true airspeed are always equal
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge defines groundspeed as the actual speed over the ground - true airspeed adjusted for wind; it decreases with a headwind and increases with a tailwind.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Magnetic variation is the angle between:
questions.explain: Variation is the angular difference between true north and magnetic north at a location.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Compass deviation is caused by:
questions.explain: Deviation is the compass error produced by the aircraft’s own electrical and metallic components.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: One degree of latitude equals approximately:
questions.explain: One degree of latitude is about 60 nautical miles (1 minute ≈ 1 NM).
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A VOR provides the pilot with:
questions.explain: A VOR gives bearing information as radials from the station; DME adds distance.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Isogonic lines on a chart connect points of equal:
questions.explain: Isogonic lines join points of equal magnetic variation (an agonic line has zero variation).
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Dead-reckoning navigation estimates position using:
questions.explain: Dead reckoning computes position from a known start using heading, speed, wind and elapsed time.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A great circle route between two points represents —
options: The shortest distance between them along the Earth's surface
questions.explain: A great circle is the shortest path between two points on a sphere; a rhumb line (constant heading) is usually longer.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: DME (Distance Measuring Equipment) provides the pilot with —
questions.explain: DME gives slant-range distance from the aircraft to the ground station, often paired with a VOR for bearing.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: An ADF (Automatic Direction Finder) needle points toward —
options: The tuned NDB station, regardless of aircraft heading
questions.explain: An ADF needle points at the tuned NDB station's relative bearing, independent of the aircraft's heading reference.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: The variation in compass reading caused by an aircraft's own magnetic and electrical equipment is corrected for using a —
questions.explain: A compass deviation card, mounted near the compass, lists correction values for the aircraft's own magnetic interference at various headings.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

banks.title: Aviation Weather
banks.desc: Atmosphere, clouds, fronts, thunderstorms, icing and visibility hazards drawn from the FAA Aviation Weather Handbook.

questions.q: What type of cloud is associated with severe turbulence, icing, lightning and hail, and should be avoided?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook identifies cumulonimbus clouds as thunderstorm clouds carrying severe turbulence, icing, lightning and hail - all thunderstorms are hazardous to aircraft.

questions.q: A thunderstorm cell transitions to the mature stage when which event occurs?
options: The downdraft cuts off the moisture supply
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook states a cell transitions to the mature stage when precipitation reaches the surface, creating a strong downdraft alongside the updraft; weather hazards peak toward the end of this stage.

questions.q: In the International Standard Atmosphere, what are the sea level temperature and pressure?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook gives the standard atmosphere as 15 degrees C (59 degrees F) and 29.92 inches of mercury (1013.25 hPa) at sea level.

questions.q: What is the standard temperature lapse rate in the troposphere?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook lists the standard tropospheric lapse rate as 6.5 degrees C per 1,000 metres, which is about 3.57 degrees F - roughly 2 degrees C per 1,000 feet.

questions.q: Which surface weather measurement is most useful for anticipating the formation of fog?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook notes the surface temperature-dewpoint spread is important in anticipating fog; when the spread reaches zero the air is saturated and fog can form.

questions.q: Radiation fog forms primarily under which conditions?
options: Strong winds and overcast skies over water
Clear skies, light winds and moist air over land at night
Warm air moving over a cold sea surface
Air being forced up sloping terrain in daytime
questions.explain: Per the FAA Aviation Weather Handbook, radiation fog is favoured by a shallow moist surface layer, clear skies and light surface winds; it is a nighttime land occurrence caused by terrestrial cooling.

questions.q: How does advection fog form?
options: Moist air moving over a colder surface and cooling below its dewpoint
Nighttime radiational cooling of the ground
Rain evaporating into cold air beneath a front
Air being lifted over a mountain ridge
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook defines advection fog as forming when moist air moves over a colder surface and is cooled below its dewpoint; it is common along coasts and is called sea fog at sea.

questions.q: A microburst is best described as which type of weather hazard?
options: A small-scale, intense downdraft - the most severe type of wind shear
A persistent layer of stratus cloud
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook describes a microburst as a small-scale, intense downdraft that spreads outward at the surface and is the most severe type of wind shear, with downdrafts up to 6,000 fpm.

questions.q: Which structural icing type is rough, milky and opaque, and is the most frequently reported icing type?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook describes rime ice as rough, milky and opaque ice formed by instantaneous freezing of small supercooled droplets, and notes it is the most frequently reported icing type.

questions.q: Which force, caused by the Earth's rotation, deflects moving air to the right in the Northern Hemisphere?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook explains the Coriolis force is an apparent force from the Earth's rotation that deflects air to the right in the Northern Hemisphere, acting at a right angle to wind direction.

questions.q: What effect does stable air generally have on the vertical motion of air?
options: It has no effect on vertical motion
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook states atmospheric stability affects vertical motion: stable air suppresses vertical motion, while unstable air enhances it.

questions.q: The arc-shaped leading edge of cool, gusty downdraft air spreading out ahead of a mature thunderstorm is called what?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook calls the arc-shaped leading edge of downdraft air a gust front; uplift along it can trigger new cells ahead of the parent cell.

questions.q: A sea breeze typically blows:
options: From land to sea by day
From sea to land by day
questions.explain: By day the land heats faster than the sea, so the surface wind (sea breeze) blows from sea to land.

questions.q: Fog forms when:
options: Air is heated above its dew point
Air is cooled to its dew point
questions.explain: Fog forms when air is cooled to its dew point and water vapour condenses near the surface.

questions.q: Structural icing requires:
options: Visible moisture and a temperature at or below freezing
questions.explain: Airframe icing needs visible moisture and a temperature at or below 0°C.

questions.q: A temperature inversion is a layer in which:
questions.explain: In an inversion, temperature increases with altitude, often trapping haze and stabilising the air.

questions.q: Wind shear is:
options: A sudden change in wind speed or direction over a short distance
questions.explain: Wind shear is a rapid change in wind velocity over a small change in position, hazardous near the ground.

questions.q: A METAR is:
options: A forecast for the next 24 hours
A routine, observed aviation weather report
questions.explain: A METAR is a routine observation of current conditions at an aerodrome (a TAF is the forecast).

questions.q: A TAF (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast) is —
options: A scheduled forecast of expected weather conditions at an aerodrome for a specified period
A record of past observed weather
questions.explain: A TAF is a forecast (not an observation) of expected aerodrome weather over a defined valid period.

questions.q: Convective SIGMETs are issued for hazards including —
options: Severe or embedded thunderstorms and tornadoes
questions.explain: Convective SIGMETs warn of severe convective weather hazards such as severe/embedded thunderstorms, tornadoes, hail and severe turbulence.

questions.q: Katabatic (drainage) winds occur when —
options: Cold, dense air flows downhill under gravity, typically at night
Warm air rises rapidly during the day
questions.explain: Katabatic winds form as cold, denser air drains downslope under gravity, most commonly overnight.

questions.q: Clear-air turbulence (CAT) is most commonly associated with —
options: Strong wind shear near the jet stream
questions.explain: CAT typically occurs in strong wind-shear zones around the jet stream, often without visible cloud to warn pilots.

banks.title: Flight Planning & Fuel
banks.desc: Mass & balance, fuel reserves, performance and the ISA — the planning numbers.
banks.source: GACAR Part 91 · FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: On a standard day (ISA), what is the sea-level temperature?
questions.explain: The ICAO Standard Atmosphere defines sea level as 15°C and 1013.25 hPa.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: What is the approximate ISA temperature lapse rate in the troposphere?
questions.explain: Temperature falls about 2°C (1.98°C) per 1,000 ft up to the tropopause.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: How does a high density altitude affect take-off performance?
questions.explain: Thinner air reduces engine thrust and lift, lengthening the take-off roll and flattening the climb.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Loading an aircraft aft of its centre-of-gravity limit mainly affects what?
questions.explain: An aft CG reduces longitudinal stability and can make stall recovery difficult.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Final reserve fuel is best described as fuel that…
options: may be used during the cruise
should never be planned for use
questions.explain: Final reserve is a protected minimum on landing — it is never planned to be consumed en route.

questions.q: One nautical mile is approximately how many metres?
questions.explain: By definition 1 NM = 1,852 m (1,609 m is a statute mile).
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: With a true airspeed of 120 kt and a 20 kt direct headwind, the groundspeed is…
questions.explain: A headwind subtracts from TAS: 120 − 20 = 100 kt groundspeed.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: To convert a fuel volume in litres to a mass, you multiply by its…
questions.explain: Mass = volume × density. Avgas density is roughly 0.72 kg/L.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A stronger headwind component has what effect on take-off and landing distance?
questions.explain: A headwind reduces the ground run required for both take-off and landing.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Density altitude equals pressure altitude when…
options: the temperature equals ISA for that level
questions.explain: When the actual temperature matches the ISA value for that level, density altitude equals pressure altitude.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Pressure altitude is the altitude read when the altimeter sub-scale is set to:
questions.explain: Pressure altitude is indicated when the altimeter is set to the standard 1013.25 hPa / 29.92 inHg.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: The crosswind component is greatest when the wind is:
questions.explain: A wind at 90° to the runway is entirely crosswind; aligned with the runway it is all headwind.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Increasing aircraft weight will:
questions.explain: Greater weight increases both take-off and landing distance and raises the stall speed.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: On a hot, high-elevation aerodrome, density altitude is:
questions.explain: High temperature raises density altitude above pressure altitude, degrading performance.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A VFR flight must plan to carry fuel to the destination plus:
questions.explain: Fuel planning must include trip fuel plus the required reserves under GACAR Part 91.

questions.q: Contingency (or extra) fuel is carried to allow for:
options: Unforeseen factors such as wind or re-routing
questions.explain: Contingency fuel covers unforeseen deviations from the plan, such as stronger winds or re-routing.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: For a fixed indicated airspeed, true airspeed:
questions.explain: As air density falls with altitude, true airspeed is higher than indicated airspeed for the same IAS.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A wind correction angle is applied to the:
options: Heading, to maintain the desired track
questions.explain: The pilot turns the heading into wind by the wind correction angle to track the desired course.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: The E6B flight computer (mechanical or electronic) is primarily used to calculate —
options: Time, speed, distance, fuel and wind-related values for flight planning
questions.explain: The E6B is a flight-planning calculator for time/speed/distance, fuel, and wind-triangle problems.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Weight and balance calculations before flight ensure the aircraft is operated —
options: Within its certificated weight and center-of-gravity limits
questions.explain: Pre-flight weight and balance planning keeps the aircraft within its certificated weight and CG envelope for safe handling.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A NOTAM should be checked during flight planning because it may report —
options: Temporary hazards or changes such as runway closures or navaid outages
questions.explain: Preflight action requires familiarity with all available information, and NOTAMs carry time-critical changes (closures, outages, hazards) not yet reflected on charts.

questions.q: "Top of descent" in flight planning is the point calculated so the aircraft —
options: Reaches the desired altitude/position at the planned descent rate
Always occurs directly over the destination airport
questions.explain: Top-of-descent planning uses a target descent rate/gradient so the aircraft reaches the desired point and altitude at the right time.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

banks.title: Radiotelephony & English Proficiency
banks.desc: The ICAO phonetic alphabet, standard phraseology and the language-proficiency requirement.

questions.q: In the ICAO phonetic alphabet, the letter 'R' is —
questions.explain: 'R' is Romeo. 'Roger' is a separate procedure word meaning the transmission was received.

questions.q: The minimum ICAO language proficiency level for international operations is —
questions.explain: ICAO Level 4 — Operational — is the minimum to exercise the privileges of a licence on international operations.

questions.q: How many skills are assessed in the ICAO language proficiency rating scale?
questions.explain: Six skills: pronunciation, structure, vocabulary, fluency, comprehension and interactions.

questions.q: The spoken word used to declare a distress condition is —
questions.explain: MAYDAY, spoken three times, declares distress — grave and imminent danger requiring immediate assistance.

questions.q: An urgency condition — a concern for safety but no immediate danger — is signalled by —
questions.explain: PAN-PAN, spoken three times, signals urgency: a condition concerning safety that does not require immediate assistance.

questions.q: The procedure word 'WILCO' means —
questions.explain: 'WILCO' — short for 'will comply' — means the message has been understood and will be complied with.

questions.q: On the radio, the number 9 is spoken as —
questions.explain: To avoid confusion with 'nein' and other words, 9 is spoken 'niner' in aviation radiotelephony.

questions.q: 'SAY AGAIN' is a request to —
options: Repeat all, or the specified part, of the last transmission
questions.explain: 'SAY AGAIN' asks the station to repeat all, or a specified part, of its last transmission.

questions.q: A read-back is specifically required for —
options: ATC route clearances and runway/level instructions
questions.explain: Safety-critical instructions — route clearances, runway and level instructions, headings and frequency changes — must be read back so ATC can confirm correct receipt.

questions.q: In the ICAO phonetic alphabet, the letter 'A' is —
questions.explain: ICAO spells the letter 'A' as 'Alfa' — deliberately with an 'f' so non-English speakers pronounce it correctly.

questions.q: An ICAO language proficiency assessment at Level 4 is valid for —
questions.explain: Level 4 is re-assessed every 3 years; Level 5 every 6 years; Level 6 (Expert) does not require re-assessment.

questions.q: The procedure word 'ROGER' means —
options: I have received all of your last transmission
questions.explain: 'ROGER' means 'I have received all of your last transmission'. It is not a substitute for 'AFFIRM' (yes) or a read-back.

questions.q: What is the minimum ICAO language proficiency rating for international operations?
questions.explain: ICAO sets Level 4 (Operational) as the minimum language proficiency for flight crew, per ICAO Annex 1.

questions.q: In the ICAO phonetic alphabet, the letter "R" is spoken as:
questions.explain: "R" is "Romeo" in the ICAO spelling alphabet (ICAO Annex 10).

questions.q: Which transponder code indicates a radio-communication failure?
questions.explain: Squawk 7600 indicates a radio (communication) failure.

questions.q: Which transponder code indicates an emergency?
questions.explain: Squawk 7700 indicates a general emergency.

questions.q: On the radio, the word "ROGER" means:
options: I have received all of your last transmission
questions.explain: "ROGER" means the transmission was received in full — it does not mean yes or that you will comply.

questions.q: On the radio, the word "WILCO" means:
options: I understand your message and will comply
questions.explain: "WILCO" means "will comply" — message understood and will be acted on.

questions.q: In radiotelephony, the word "AFFIRM" means —
questions.explain: AFFIRM is the standard ICAO procedure word for "yes" or "permission granted / that is correct."

questions.q: In radiotelephony, the word "NEGATIVE" means —
options: No / permission not granted / that is not correct
questions.explain: NEGATIVE means "no", "permission not granted", or "that is not correct."

questions.q: The procedure word "STANDBY" means —
options: Wait and I will call you
questions.explain: STANDBY tells the receiving station to wait; the calling station will call again.

questions.q: To avoid confusion with similar-sounding words, the number "3" is pronounced in radiotelephony as —
questions.explain: ICAO radiotelephony spelling pronounces "3" as "TREE" to reduce ambiguity for non-native speakers.

questions.q: The number "5" is pronounced in radiotelephony as —
questions.explain: ICAO radiotelephony pronounces "5" as "FIFE."

questions.q: The ICAO document that expands on the Annex 1 language proficiency requirements, including the rating scale descriptors, is —
questions.explain: ICAO Doc 9835, the Manual on the Implementation of ICAO Language Proficiency Requirements, expands on Annex 1's rating scale.
